title: AI Model Sovereignty Goes Bidirectional as China Eyes Export Controls While US Clears GPT-5.6
subhead: Both the US and China are now restricting access to their most powerful AI models, DeepSeek is building its own inference chip, and the Pentagon starts stockpiling lithium as the tech-power landscape fragments further.

sections.heading: China Considers Restricting Access to Its Top AI Models
sections.body:
Reuters reports that Beijing is weighing restrictions on overseas access to China's most advanced AI models, including those currently available as open-source. Chinese authorities have held talks with major firms including Alibaba and ByteDance about tightening controls on how their frontier models are distributed internationally. The move mirrors the US government's own restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models, which were temporarily pulled from the market last month under a Commerce Department export control directive.

According to Reuters, Beijing has also discussed using China's national security laws against anyone accused of stealing or leaking proprietary Chinese AI technology. A Reuters Breakingviews analysis notes that confining models from $92 billion Zhipu and other Chinese AI firms behind export walls would carry significant economic costs, potentially isolating Chinese firms from the global developer ecosystem that has driven their adoption.

The timing is notable: China's move comes just as the US has cleared OpenAI's GPT-5.6 for broad public release and restored access to Anthropic's restricted models. Both sides are now treating frontier AI as a strategic asset subject to export control, marking a significant escalation from the semiconductor-focused restrictions of recent years.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI sovereignty race is no longer one-sided. Both Washington and Beijing are now restricting frontier model access, which means the era of globally available open-source frontier AI may be ending — and with it, the assumption that cutting-edge AI will remain a freely traded commodity.

sections.heading: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Gets Broad Release as US AI Regulation Stays Voluntary
sections.body:
OpenAI will publicly launch GPT-5.6 on July 9, roughly two weeks after initially limiting the model to a small group of trusted partners at the US government's request. The model comes in three variants: Sol (flagship), Terra (mid-tier, half the cost of Sol), and Luna (budget). According to Axios and CNBC, the Department of Commerce's Center for AI Standards and Innovation conducted additional testing before clearing the broader release.

However, a White House official pushed back on characterizing this as formal approval, stating that 'no such permission is granted or required' and citing Trump's June 2 executive order that bars mandatory federal licensing for AI models. The contradiction is telling: the government pressured OpenAI into a staggered release, then publicly insisted it didn't need to. OpenAI also proposed offering the US government a 5% equity stake in the company, according to Yahoo News.

Meanwhile, Anthropic has restored access to its Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models after Commerce lifted export restrictions late last month. Anthropic extended free Fable 5 access through July 12 for paid subscribers before shifting to a prepaid credits model, according to Android Authority.
sections.whyItMatters: The US approach to frontier AI regulation is evolving through ad hoc executive pressure rather than legislation. Companies are navigating government 'voluntary' consultation processes that feel de facto mandatory, creating an unpredictable compliance environment that advantages well-connected incumbents.

sections.heading: DeepSeek Builds Its Own Inference Chip to Reduce Nvidia and Huawei Dependence
sections.body:
Chinese AI startup DeepSeek is developing its own AI inference chip, according to three sources familiar with the matter who spoke to The Hindu and Reuters. The chip is designed for inference — running trained models — rather than training, targeting the fastest-growing segment of AI compute demand. The effort began about a year ago, and DeepSeek has been reaching out to external partners in chip design, foundry, and memory.

DeepSeek has also been quietly hiring chip-design engineers in recent months, two sources said. The company is simultaneously raising $7 billion in its first external funding round at a valuation of $52-59 billion, Reuters reported in June — a dramatic reversal from its longstanding refusal of outside capital.

If successful, DeepSeek would join a growing list of AI companies pursuing custom silicon. OpenAI unveiled its custom inference chip 'Jalapeno' (developed with Broadcom) last month, and Anthropic has been weighing building its own chips. DeepSeek's case is different: US export controls bar it from accessing Nvidia's most advanced chips, and while it has leaned on Huawei's Ascend chips for its V4 model, Huawei's hold on China's ~$50 billion domestic AI chip market is already weakening as Alibaba and Baidu develop their own chips.

However, major hurdles remain. US restrictions cut Chinese firms off from the most advanced overseas foundries and high-bandwidth memory critical for inference chips. Analyst Richard Windsor of Radio Free Mobile noted that DeepSeek has 'almost no chance of selling silicon outside of China unless it gets access to leading edge manufacturing.'
sections.whyItMatters: DeepSeek's chip push signals that China's top AI firms are no longer willing to rely on Huawei as a domestic chip supplier, even as US export controls make self-sufficiency extraordinarily difficult. The inference chip race is becoming a second front in the semiconductor sovereignty battle.

sections.heading: SambaNova Raises $1 Billion at $11 Billion Valuation in Inference Chip Surge
sections.body:
US-based AI chip startup SambaNova raised $1 billion in a Series F round led by General Atlantic, reaching an $11 billion valuation. Intel Capital, Vista Equity Partners, and JPMorgan Chase participated. The round follows a $350 million raise in February, when SambaNova debuted its SN50 inference chip — which the company claims delivers 3x the throughput of Nvidia's B200 at 5x the top speed.

SambaNova's SN50 uses a tile-based architecture that pairs processing circuits with high-speed SRAM memory on each tile, reducing data travel time. The chip includes HBM memory for active model weights and a DRAM module for swapping between multiple AI models in milliseconds. A 16-chip appliance, the SambaRack SN50, runs on roughly 20 watts per chip and uses standard air cooling rather than the liquid cooling required by many high-end GPU servers.

JPMorgan, one of the round's participants, separately announced plans to adopt the SN50 and its predecessor SN40 for on-premises inference infrastructure — a notable enterprise commitment for a chip startup. The funding signals that investors see real commercial traction in inference-specific silicon as AI workloads shift from training to running models.
sections.whyItMatters: SambaNova's $1 billion round and JPMorgan adoption show that the inference chip market is maturing fast. As AI compute demand shifts from training to running models, purpose-built inference silicon is attracting serious capital — and giving enterprises an alternative to Nvidia's general-purpose GPUs.

sections.heading: Pentagon Moves to Stockpile $300 Million of Lithium as Critical Minerals Race Intensifies
sections.body:
The US Department of Defense's Defense Logistics Agency is offering up to $300 million to purchase more than 35 million pounds of battery-grade lithium carbonate over the next five years, according to a June 2 solicitation posted on SAM.gov and reported by E&E News and Bloomberg. The solicitation is part of a broader Pentagon push to secure and produce critical minerals that China largely controls.

The funding comes from the GOP megabill that set aside $2 billion for the Pentagon to stockpile minerals. Separately, Canadian company Largo announced a $60 million Pentagon contract to provide vanadium pentoxide for energy storage systems.

This coincides with Japan's push at the G7 summit for mineral stockpiling. According to SCMP, Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi pressed G7 allies on critical minerals security, seeking to bridge divides as the alliance grapples with supply chain vulnerability to Chinese processing dominance.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is treating critical minerals as a defense procurement problem, not just a trade policy one. The Pentagon's direct purchasing of lithium and vanadium marks a shift from indirect subsidies to outright stockpiling — a sign that Washington is preparing for supply disruptions, not just market inefficiencies.

sections.heading: SpaceX Files for 100,000-Starlink Mega-Constellation as NATO Plans Military Satellite Network
sections.body:
SpaceX filed a formal application with the FCC on July 6 for permission to orbit up to 100,000 Gen3 Starlink satellites. The next-generation satellites, also called V3, would weigh up to 2,000 kg each (compared to ~575 kg for Gen2), support up to 1 Tbps downlink and 4 Tbps combined backhaul capacity per satellite, and operate in very-low Earth orbit at 323-477.5 km altitude. SpaceX would need Starship to launch them, tying the constellation's future to the still-developing heavy-lift vehicle.

The filing comes the same week SpaceX marked its 1,000th Falcon 9 launch and the Starlink fleet passed 12,000 active satellites, according to MSN. The Gen3 plan would represent an order-of-magnitude expansion of the world's largest satellite constellation.

Meanwhile, eight NATO countries plan to link their military satellites into a 'mega-constellation' for high-speed secure communications, Breaking Defense reports. And in a separate milestone, Miami-based City Labs launched the BOHR satellite — the world's first commercial nuclear-powered payload in orbit, using a tritium betavoltaic battery. While the power output is in the nanowatt-to-microwatt range, Ars Technica reports it represents the first commercial nuclear mission to pass the FAA's new nuclear launch approval process, opening a path for future nuclear-powered spacecraft.
sections.whyItMatters: SpaceX's 100,000-satellite filing and NATO's military mega-constellation plan show orbital real estate becoming a contested strategic domain. City Labs' nuclear-powered satellite milestone adds another dimension: commercial actors are now entering the nuclear space domain that was previously government-only territory.

sections.heading: Chinese Lidar in US Infrastructure: Hesai's Expanding Reach Despite Blacklist
sections.body:
A CNBC investigation highlights growing concerns over Hesai Technology, a Shanghai-based lidar manufacturer that was blacklisted as a national security threat by the US Department of Defense in 2024. Despite the blacklist designation — which prevents Hesai from securing Pentagon contracts but does not ban commercial use — the company's sensors are being integrated into expanding US infrastructure and autonomous systems.

Hesai has an expanded partnership with Nvidia to supply lidar for its autonomous vehicle platforms. Its sensors are used by Amazon's Zoox robotaxis, autonomous trucking companies Waabi and Kodiak, and even at New York's JFK International Airport for monitoring passenger and traffic flow. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has called robotics and self-driving vehicles the company's second most important growth category.

Craig Singleton of the Foundation for Defense of Democracies warns that Chinese lidar sensors moving closer to defense nodes, utility grids, and airports could be 'weaponized by a hostile foreign power.' Hesai's SEC filings disclose that the Chinese government has 'significant oversight in regulating our operations and may influence or intervene in our operations at any time.' Hesai CEO David Li denies the security concerns, saying the sensors lack memory capacity to store data.

The situation mirrors the earlier Huawei 5G saga, where US companies initially embraced low-cost Chinese technology only to spend billions on a 'rip and replace' program after security concerns escalated.
sections.whyItMatters: Hesai's case shows that the US-China tech security frontier has moved beyond 5G and chips into the physical AI infrastructure layer — sensors and autonomous systems. The blacklist-only approach leaves a regulatory gap: US commercial entities can still deploy blacklisted Chinese technology in critical infrastructure, and the current framework has no mechanism to stop them.

bottomLine: The defining development this week is the bidirectional closing of the AI model frontier: the US cleared GPT-5.6 for public release after weeks of de facto export controls on Anthropic's models, while China is now considering its own export restrictions on advanced AI models including open-source ones. Simultaneously, DeepSeek's move to build custom inference chips and SambaNova's $1 billion raise confirm that the battle for AI compute is shifting from training to inference, with both Chinese and Western firms racing to reduce dependence on Nvidia. The Pentagon's $300 million lithium stockpiling initiative and SpaceX's 100,000-satellite filing underscore that the strategic competition is expanding from chips and models into minerals and orbital real estate.
sources: Reuters, CNBC, The Hindu, SiliconANGLE, E&E News, Bloomberg, Ars Technica, Breaking Defense, onMSFT, Android Authority, Firstpost, IAPP, SCMP, Yahoo News, MSN, VentureBurn, Advanced Television, New Atlas, White House Fact Sheet
